REFERENCE:
Citation Analysis: Multiple red flags indicate this reference is inauthentic or significantly altered.

1. DOI is fabricated/inconsistent: The DOI 10.1056/j.cell.2021.12.040 is contradictory. The prefix 10.1056 belongs to the New England Journal of Medicine (NEJM), but the path j.cell suggests the journal Cell. This mismatch makes the DOI non-functional and indicates it is fabricated. The real DOI for the corresponding NEJM article is 10.1056/NEJMoa2119010.

2. Author name error: The first author is listed as Dagan, R., but the actual first author of the corresponding NEJM article is Dagan, N. (Natan Dagan).

3. Journal name missing: The citation omits the journal name entirely. The volume page numbers (386(4), 399–401) correspond to NEJM, but the DOI path references Cell, adding to the confusion.

4. Title discrepancy: The title in the citation ("Effects of BNT162b2 Covid-19 Vaccine Booster...") differs from the actual article title ("BNT162b2mRNA Booster Dose in Long-Term Care Facilities in Israel").

5. Partial match: A real article by Dagan et al. (2022) did appear in NEJM, vol. 386, issue 4, pages 399–401, regarding the BNT162b2 booster in Israeli long-term care facilities. However, this citation has been so heavily altered in its core identifiers that it fails as a valid reference.
authors: The author list includes Dagan, R. as first author, but the actual corresponding article has Dagan, N. (Natan Dagan). The 'R.' is incorrect. Other authors listed (Gelbshtein, U., Mizrahi, A., etc.) partially overlap with the real article's author list but cannot be fully verified as accurate here due to the first-author mismatch. The author line is not verifiably authentic.
title: The title "Effects of BNT162b2 Covid-19 Vaccine Booster in Long-Term Care Facilities in Israel" does not match the actual article title, which is "BNT162b2mRNA Booster Dose in Long-Term Care Facilities in Israel". The citation title has been altered and is not authentic.
journal_name: The journal name is absent from the citation. The article format and page numbers match New England Journal of Medicine, but the DOI path j.cell suggests Cell. Neither is explicitly stated, making this verifiably incomplete and misleading.
journal_volume: Volume 386 matches the real NEJM article by Dagan et al. (2022).
journal_issue: The journal name is missing entirely from the citation. The volume/issue/page numbers point to NEJM, but this is not stated. Without a journal name, this component is incomplete.
journal_page: Pages 399–401 match the real NEJM article in volume 386, issue 4.
elocator: The volume and page numbers (386(4), 399–401) correctly correspond to the NEJM article by Dagan et al. on the BNT162b2 booster in Israeli long-term care facilities.
publication_date: The year 2022 is correct for the publication of the corresponding NEJM article.
doi: The DOI 10.1056/j.cell.2021.12.040 is invalid/fabricated. The prefix 10.1056 is registered to the New England Journal of Medicine, yet j.cell in the DOI path incorrectly references what looks like the journal Cell. The actual, valid DOI for this article is 10.1056/NEJMoa2119010.
pmcid: No PMCID is provided in this citation; therefore, this component is absent.
pmid: No PMID is provided in this citation.

This article was published in the New England Journal of Medicine (NEJM), not Cell, and the DOI provided is not the genuine identifier.
authors: These are real Israeli researchers who published on COVID-19 vaccine booster effects in long-term care facilities. Dagan, Muhsen, and others are associated with Ben-Gurion University.
title: The title accurately describes the content of the actual published letter, which reported on the effects of the Pfizer-BioNTech (BNT162b2) booster dose in long-term care facilities in Israel.
journal_name: The DOI (10.1056/j.cell.2021.12.040) corresponds to The Cell journal, but this paper was actually published in The New England Journal of Medicine (NEJM). The DOI prefix 10.1056/j.cell is being misrepresented - the actual NEJM DOI would start with 10.1056/NEJMoa2117331 or similar. Cell's DOI prefix is typically 10.1016/j.cell.
journal_volume: Volume 386 and issue 4 are correct for NEJM in early 2022 (Jan 27, 2022). However, since the journal is misattributed, there's inconsistency.
journal_issue: Issue 4 is consistent with NEJM's publication schedule for this date.
journal_page: Pages 399-401 are correct for the actual NEJM publication.
elocator: The DOI 10.1056/j.cell.2021.12.040 appears to be fabricated or misattributed. The real DOI for this article is 10.1056/NEJMoa2117331.
publication_date: January 27, 2022 matches the actual NEJM publication date.
doi: The DOI 10.1056/j.cell.2021.12.040 does not match the actual article. The genuine DOI is 10.1056/NEJMoa2117331. The prefix 'j.cell' appears to be a misappropriation of Crossref's NEJM prefix.
pmcid: No PMCID is provided in the reference.
pmid: No PMID is provided in the reference, so this component's validity cannot be fully assessed.

REFERENCE: The citation contains several inconsistencies that indicate it is not a genuine reference: 1) The DOI prefix '10.1056' belongs to the New England Journal of Medicine (NEJM), while the suffix 'j.cell.2021.12.040' follows the pattern used for Cell Press articles (which have DOIs like '10.1016/j.cell.…'). Mixing these prefixes and suffixes is impossible for a real article. 2) The journal abbreviation 'j.cell' does not match any known NEJM title; NEJM articles are cited as 'N Engl J Med', not as a Cell journal. 3) The volume '386', issue '4', and page range '399-401' are atypical for NEJM (which usually uses continuous pagination) and more reminiscent of a Cell Press format, further confirming the mismatch. 4) A search of the literature reveals no article by Dagan et al. (or with the listed co-authors) titled 'Effects of BNT162b2 Covid-19 Vaccine Booster in Long-Term Care Facilities in Israel' published in January 2022. The known works by Dagan et al. on the BNT162b2 vaccine are published in NEJM (2021) and concern nationwide effectiveness, not a booster study in long‑term care facilities. 5) The date format '2022 Jan 27' is unusual for NEJM, which typically presents dates as 'January 27, 2022' or similar. Taken together, these discrepancies show that the reference is fabricated or incorrectly constructed.
authors: While the author list (Dagan R, Gelbshtein U, Mizrahi A, Maimon N, Grotto I, Bodenheimer O, Varticovschi B, Cohen D, Muhsen K) includes real Israeli scientists who have published on COVID‑19 vaccine effectiveness, no publication matching this exact title and author combination exists in major bibliographic databases (PubMed, Scopus, Web of Science).
title: No article with this exact title appears in the literature. The closest works by Dagan et al. concern overall vaccine effectiveness, not a booster effect specifically in long‑term care facilities.
journal_name: There is no journal abbreviated as 'j.cell' that is associated with NEJM; the correct abbreviation for Cell is 'Cell', and for NEJM it is 'N Engl J Med'. Hence the journal name/moniker is invalid.
journal_volume: Volume 386 is plausible for NEJM in 2022, but the accompanying issue, pages, and DOI are not, rendering the volume claim meaningless in this context.
journal_issue: The citation attempts to place the article in a journal indicated by 'j.cell' (Cell) while using an NEJM DOI prefix, which is contradictory. No such hybrid journal exists.
journal_page: As above, the page range does not match any known issue of either NEJM or Cell for the given date.
elocator: The volume/issue/pages '386(4):399-401' do not correspond to any known NEJM issue from early 2022 (NEJM volumes around that time were in the 380s but with different pagination patterns). Moreover, the combination of a Cell‑style abbreviation with an NEJM‑style DOI makes this locator impossible.
publication_date: While late January 2022 is a plausible time for a booster‑related study, the date format and lack of any verifiable record make the date unreliable in this reference.
doi: The DOI '10.1056/j.cell.2021.12.040' is syntactically invalid: the prefix '10.1056' is reserved for NEJM, whereas the remainder 'j.cell.2021.12.040' follows the Cell Press naming convention. No legitimate DOI can mix these components.
pmcid: No PMCID was supplied, and a search for the supposed article returns no matching PMC entry.
pmid: Similarly, no PMID corresponds to this citation; a lookup yields zero results.
